---

## Figure Type

This is a **borehole/well log (каротажная диаграмма)** — a composite geophysical well log from a Soviet-era drilling program. It appears to be a multi-track wireline log displayed on a long vertical strip format, typical of Soviet exploration practice. The log includes what appear to be **multiple geophysical curves** (likely resistivity, spontaneous potential/SP, and possibly gamma-ray or induction curves) plotted alongside a **lithological column** and associated data tables. At the bottom there is a separate section labeled as a **контрольный приём** (control/repeat section), which was standard Soviet practice for verifying log quality.

---

## Coverage and Subject

The figure covers a single **borehole (скважина)**, identified in the header area. The title at the top reads (partially legible):

**"Уч-к ТАСТЫ-ТАЛДЫ"** — indicating the survey area/site is called **Tasty-Taldy** (Тасты-Талды), a toponym in northern Kazakhstan.

- **Report/survey number**: appears to reference a survey designation in the upper left [partially illegible]
- **Borehole number**: [illegible from scan quality, likely скв-№ XX]
- The log covers a substantial **depth interval**, likely several hundred metres based on the length of the strip, though precise depth values on the depth track are [illegible at this resolution]
- The geological column on the left margin shows **multiple lithological units** differentiated by standard Soviet hatching/pattern symbols (indicating alternating rock types — likely sandstones, clays, siltstones, and possibly harder crystalline or carbonate units)

## Key Content

**Lithological Column (left margin):**
- A narrow column with standard Soviet lithological patterns is visible running the full length of the log
- Multiple distinct units are shown using different hatch patterns, suggesting interbedded sedimentary sequences with at least one zone of notably different character (possibly a harder or crystalline basement section in the lower portion)
- Pattern symbols consistent with: clays/mudstones (horizontal lines), sandstones (dotted), siltstones (mixed), and possibly volcanic or crystalline rocks in deeper sections

**Geophysical Curves (main body — two principal track groups):**
- **Left curve track**: Shows a strongly deflecting curve with high-amplitude oscillations throughout much of the section. The curve has prominent **spikes and deflections** particularly in the upper-middle portion of the log, suggesting zones of contrasting resistivity or SP response. There appear to be **two curves** plotted (possibly KS — кажущееся сопротивление/apparent resistivity — and PS — потенциал самополяризации/SP), one with larger amplitude excursions
- **Right curve track**: A second set of curves with similar but distinct character, likely a different electrode spacing or a different geophysical method (possibly gradient vs. potential resistivity arrays, or a radioactivity log)
- Several **notable anomalous zones** are visible where both curves show strong deflections simultaneously — these are the most geologically significant intervals and likely correspond to permeable/porous beds, mineralized zones, or lithological contacts
- In approximately the middle depth range, there is a zone where the left track shows **particularly large amplitude deflections** — this may correspond to a key formation or mineralized interval
- The lower portion of the log shows somewhat more subdued curve responses

**Annotations on log:**
- At least one **formation name or marker** is annotated mid-log: partially legible text appears to read **"Горизонт №..."** or a similar formation/horizon designation [illegible]
- Depth markers appear along the left edge at regular intervals [values illegible at this resolution]

**Control Section (bottom):**
- Labeled **"КОНТРОЛЬНЫЙ ПРИЁМ"** — the repeat/control run section, covering a shallower portion of the borehole re-logged to verify instrument calibration and repeatability
- The control curves show reasonable correspondence to the main log curves, suggesting acceptable log quality

**Right-side data table:**
- A narrow columnar table occupies the right margin, likely containing **core recovery, formation picks, or lithological descriptions** by depth interval [detail illegible]

## Research Value

This borehole log provides direct subsurface lithological and petrophysical data for the **Tasty-Taldy site** in northern Kazakhstan, allowing geologists to correlate formation boundaries, identify permeable or mineralized intervals through resistivity/SP anomalies, and calibrate geophysical survey interpretations against actual drilled stratigraphy. The prominent curve deflections at multiple depth intervals are key targets for identifying zones of elevated resistivity (potentially associated with ore mineralization, tight carbonates, or basement rocks) or reduced resistivity (clays, brines, altered zones). In the context of Report 36572's regional geophysical survey, this log would anchor subsurface interpretations of seismic and potential-field data to real formation depths and properties, directly informing assessments of mineral or hydrocarbon potential at this specific location.
